planting.note: Direct sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow two or three seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil once frost danger has passed, thinning to the strongest seedling per hill. These white-skinned fruits reach 12 to 15 pounds and hold their clean color well after harvest, so give vines plenty of room to sprawl. The strong, sturdy handle makes them ideal for carving and display. Intermediate powdery mildew resistance helps vines stay productive, but water at the base and space plants for airflow to further reduce disease pressure.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before your last frost, keeping the mix at 70 to 85 degrees for quick germination. This 1950s heirloom is a determinate type, so most of its 6 to 8 ounce, low-acid fruits ripen over a concentrated window, making it excellent for canning. Transplant seedlings deeply once nights stay warm, and stake or cage the compact bushes to keep fruit off the ground. Harvest tomatoes when fully colored and slightly soft for the best fresh flavor.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring once soil is workable; parsnips need a long season.

directions: Sow fresh seed 1/2 inch deep directly in loose, stone-free soil, since parsnips do not transplant well and seed is short-lived. Germination is slow, often two to four weeks, so keep the bed evenly moist and mark the row. This variety thickens early, maturing in as little as 95 days, producing tender white roots with a sweet, distinctive flavor. Flavor sweetens further after a fall frost, so leave roots in the ground and harvest as needed through cold weather.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches at least 65 degrees.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm soil, planting in blocks of at least four short rows rather than a single long row to ensure good pollination. This bicolor sugar-enhanced hybrid produces 8-inch ears of tender, creamy kernels and offers early vigor plus strong disease resistance. Feed with nitrogen and water deeply during tasseling and ear fill. Harvest when silks brown and kernels release a milky juice when punctured, and refrigerate quickly to preserve sweetness.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable, or again in late summer.

directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep in cool soil in early spring or late summer, thinning seedlings to give each plant room. This 1952 All-America Selections winner has thick, savoyed leaves and unusual tolerance to both heat and drought, extending the harvest window. It performs well in the ground or in containers, so it suits small gardens. Harvest outer leaves as needed, or cut the whole plant before it bolts in warm weather.

planting.note: Direct sow in mid to late summer for a fall harvest after frost sweetens roots.

directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep in loose, fertile soil about three months before your first fall frost, thinning to 6 inches apart. Roots grow 4 to 6 inches long with sweet, mellow yellow flesh that mashes like potatoes or adds body to soups and stews. The tender tops are also edible as sauteed greens. Rutabagas develop the best flavor in cool weather, so leave them in the ground until after a light frost before harvesting.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost for spring, or midsummer for fall.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before transplanting, then set out sturdy seedlings in rich, evenly moist soil. This variety forms striking purple, domed heads that mature early and reliably in spring or fall and hold color best with steady moisture. Vigorous plants tolerate a range of climates and perform especially well in the Eastern US. Harvest heads while the curds are still tight, and enjoy the tender, flavorful florets steamed or roasted.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost, keeping the mix warm for even germination. This indeterminate heirloom produces meaty, acorn-shaped red fruits of 8 to 12 ounces with few seeds, excellent for rich sauce and paste but also good for slicing. Provide a tall stake or trellis and prune suckers to manage the vigorous vines. Water consistently at the base to prevent cracking and blossom-end rot, and pick fruit as it colors fully.

planting.note: Start indoors 6 to 8 weeks before last frost; transplant once nights stay warm.

directions: Start seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, keeping the mix at 75 to 90 degrees since peppers germinate slowly in cool soil. Transplant into warm, well-drained soil after nights are reliably above 55 degrees. The 3 to 6 inch green poblano is excellent stuffed for chiles rellenos, while the dried, reddish-brown ancho anchors sauces like mole poblano, with mild heat around 1,000 to 2,000 Scoville units. Harvest green for fresh use or leave fruit to ripen red for drying.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil once frost danger has passed, spacing hills to give vines room to run. Prolific plants yield up to fifteen uniform fruits weighing 1.5 to 2 pounds each, sized perfectly for serving on the half-shell. When cooked, the flesh forks away in tender spaghetti-like strands. Cure harvested fruit in a warm, dry spot for a week or two to improve storage life, and keep on the vine until the rind hardens.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep directly in warm soil, since beans resent transplanting and rot in cold ground. This vigorous variety produces 5 to 5.5 inch pods that stay tender and crisp even as the season heats up. Sturdy, upright plants hold pods off the soil for clean, easy picking and do well in containers. Harvest frequently while pods are young and snap crisply to keep plants producing over a long window.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost, or grow indoors year-round for baby greens.

directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep and thin lightly, or broadcast densely for a cut-and-come-again baby-greens crop. This chard blend produces colorful young leaves ideal in salads or steamed, stir-fried, and added to soups, omelets, and lasagna. It can be grown indoors any time of year for a fresh winter harvest. Snip outer leaves when a few inches tall and the plants will regrow for repeated cuttings.

common_name: Artisan Bumble Bee Blend Pole Cherry Tomato

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This blend combines Pink, Purple, and Sunrise Bumble Bee cherry tomatoes, each striped and no two alike, sweet and tangy right off the vine. The indeterminate plants grow large and yield heavily, so provide sturdy support and prune suckers for airflow. Water evenly to reduce cracking, and harvest fruit as it colors fully throughout the season.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or late summer, or grow indoors year-round.

directions: Broadcast seed thickly about 1/4 inch deep for a dense baby-greens crop, sowing every couple of weeks for a steady supply. Peppery leaves are rich in vitamin C and potassium and taste great with balsamic and Parmesan. This crop grows quickly in cool weather and can be raised indoors year-round for fresh winter greens. Cut leaves when 2 to 3 inches tall and the bed will often regrow for a second harvest.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for fall harvest.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool weather and thin for full-size plants, or leave dense for baby greens ready in about 20 days. The peppery, mustardy leaves brighten salads and are also good steamed or on pizza. Grow in full sun to part shade and keep soil moist to slow bolting, which turns leaves bitter and hot in heat. Succession sow every two to three weeks in spring and fall for a continuous harvest.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall; this perennial-type arugula grows slowly.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool soil; wild rocket grows more slowly than common arugula but offers a stronger, more complex peppery flavor. Harvest as baby greens or let plants reach full size for salads, pizza, or pesto. It grows well in containers and tolerates part shade, and the deeply lobed leaves regrow after cutting. Keep soil evenly moist and harvest regularly to keep leaves tender and slow flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost for spring, or midsummer for fall.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before setting out sturdy transplants into rich, evenly moist soil. This hybrid combines the mild, sweet flavor of broccoli with tender, asparagus-like stems and matures quickly. After the first central shoots, plants keep producing slender side shoots for multiple harvests. Cut stems when buds are still tight and harvest often to encourage continued production; steam or stir-fry the whole tender stem.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or late summer for a long, cool-season harvest.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool soil and thin for full plants or leave dense for baby greens ready in about a month. This variety has wider leaves and is slower to bolt than other arugulas, giving more leaf per harvest and a milder, peppery flavor. Keep soil moist and grow in part shade during warm spells to delay bolting. Cut outer leaves regularly, and succession sow for a steady supply through spring and fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes; this giant needs a very long, warm season.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, deeply enriched soil and give each plant a very large area to sprawl. Under ideal conditions Atlantic Giant can reach record-breaking size, and it is the classic choice of competitive growers. For maximum size, allow just one pumpkin per vine, feed heavily, and water generously and consistently. Even without a record, you will have plenty of flesh for pies and soups or a dramatic carving pumpkin.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring once soil is workable, with successions every three weeks.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in loose, stone-free soil and keep the bed evenly moist until the slow seedlings emerge. This imperator-type carrot grows 8 to 10 inches long with a brilliant deep-red exterior and orange core, and roasting or steaming intensifies the color contrast. Thin seedlings to 2 to 3 inches so roots size up well. Sow successive crops every three weeks for a continuous supply of these colorful, sweet, crisp carrots.

planting.note: Direct sow or start indoors in early spring, and again in late summer for fall.

directions: Sow seed just 1/8 inch deep and barely cover, since lettuce needs light to germinate and prefers cool soil. This easy variety forms bright green leaves dusted with iridescent bronze tips and combines the buttery texture of a butterhead with the upright habit of a mini romaine. Keep soil consistently moist and provide afternoon shade in warm weather to delay bolting. Harvest outer leaves as needed or cut the whole head once it fills out.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring and again in late summer; heat can trigger bolting.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool, fertile soil and thin to about 5 inches apart, or grow in containers. This Asian green, also called pak choi, is ready in about 35 days when harvested at 5 to 6 inches tall for tender baby choi. It resists bolting and makes an attractive addition to mixed containers. Keep soil evenly moist to prevent bolting, and harvest whole heads or outer stalks for stir-fries and marinated salads.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and keep the mix warm for even sprouting. This century-old indeterminate heirloom produces thick, meaty slices prized for sandwiches and any dish wanting rich tomato flavor. Vines are extremely vigorous, reaching 6 feet or more, so provide a sturdy cage or wire support and prune suckers for airflow. Water evenly at the base to prevent cracking and blossom-end rot, and harvest fruit fully colored.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This determinate type delivers a rich, umami-packed flavor that finishes with smoky, fire-roasted notes, ripening most of its crop over a concentrated window. Stake or cage the compact bushes to keep fruit clean and support the yield. Water consistently at the base to avoid cracking, and harvest tomatoes when fully colored and slightly soft.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost for spring, or midsummer for fall.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before setting out transplants into rich, evenly moist soil. Selected for heat tolerance, this variety suits both spring and late-summer sowings and forms large, 6 to 8 inch, tightly packed blue-green heads. After the main head is cut, many smaller side shoots follow for continued harvests, and it grows well through winter in mild climates. Cut the central head while buds are tight, then keep harvesting side shoots.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and keep the mix warm for even germination. This hybrid crosses Big Dwarf with Brandywine to yield dark pink, ribbed fruits of 12 to 15 ounces with rich flavor and productive, nearly blemish-free yields. Provide a tall stake or trellis for the indeterminate vines and prune suckers for airflow. Water evenly at the base to prevent cracking, and harvest tomatoes when fully colored.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes; needs a long, warm growing season.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, richly amended soil and give each vine ample space to run. This heirloom is a favorite of giant-pumpkin growers, producing round fruits with smooth, light-orange skin well suited to Halloween carving and decorating. For maximum size, grow one pumpkin per vine and water and feed generously through the season. Leave fruit on the vine until the rind hardens, then cut with a length of stem attached.

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer or fall; extremely cold-hardy for winter harvest.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in cool soil in late summer or fall, since mache germinates best in cool conditions. This big-seeded variety tolerates heat better than others without turning bitter and is also very cold-hardy, often surviving to about 10 degrees. Enjoy the tender rosettes alone as a salad, mixed with other greens, or lightly cooked like spinach. Harvest whole rosettes at the base as needed through cool and cold weather.

planting.note: Start indoors 6 to 8 weeks before last frost; transplant once nights stay warm.

directions: Start seed indoors 6 to 8 weeks before the last frost, keeping the mix at 75 to 90 degrees for reliable germination. This standard-setting 1902 heirloom produces large, high-quality fruit with tender skin that needs no peeling, and its lavender blossoms are ornamental. Transplant into warm, well-drained soil after nights stay above 55 degrees. Harvest fruit while the skin is glossy and firm, cutting with pruners since stems are tough, and pick regularly to keep plants productive.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil once frost danger has passed, thinning to the strongest plants per hill. This highly productive bush-type zucchini from the 1920s takes little space and yields dark green, glossy fruits with tender skin. Harvest fruit young, around 6 to 8 inches, for the best texture and to keep plants producing. Pick every day or two at peak season, as fruit sizes up fast and overgrown squash slows further set.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm soil once frost danger has passed, giving vines room to sprawl. These dramatic, deeply ribbed pumpkins darken as they mature until nearly black and weigh only 1 to 2 pounds, making tidy personal-sized decorations. Group several together for striking fall displays. Leave fruit on the vine until the rind hardens and color fully develops, then cut with a bit of stem attached for the best keeping quality.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This Russian heirloom produces baseball-sized, 10 to 12 ounce fruits with reddish-brown flesh and a rich, slightly salty flavor, and it sets fruit well in heat. Provide a sturdy stake or cage for the indeterminate vines and prune suckers for airflow. Water evenly to reduce cracking, and harvest when shoulders take on their characteristic dusky color.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Sow seed just 1/8 inch deep and barely cover, since lettuce needs light to germinate in cool soil. A garden favorite since 1850, this loose-leaf variety yields full-sized, frilly leaves in just over a month and baby greens in about three weeks. It withstands hot, dry conditions and light frosts better than many other lettuces. Harvest outer leaves as needed for a cut-and-come-again supply, or cut the whole plant before it bolts in heat.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring as soon as soil is workable, or again in late summer.

directions: Sow seed 1/2 inch deep in cool soil in early spring or late summer, thinning to give each plant room. This dependable open-pollinated variety from the early 1800s has crinkled, savoyed leaves and can be harvested as tender baby greens in as little as 28 days. It performs well in the ground or in containers and tolerates part shade. Cut outer leaves as needed or harvest whole plants before warm weather triggers bolting.

planting.note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: Separate bulbs into cloves at planting and set each clove pointed end up, 2 inches deep, in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes. This Turban-type hardneck forms large, plump bulbs with violet-streaked wrappers and 8 to 10 easy-peel cloves. Mulch after planting to protect roots over winter, and cut off the curling scapes in early summer to direct energy into the bulbs. Harvest when lower leaves brown, then cure in a dry, airy place before storing.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep directly in warm soil, since beans dislike transplanting and rot in cold ground. This variety produces a very large concentrated crop of round, 6 inch pods on 16 to 18 inch bushy, disease-resistant plants. Because it crops all at once, sow in successions two weeks apart for a steady supply. Harvest pods while young and tender before the seeds swell, and pick frequently to encourage more pods.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil has warmed to at least 65 degrees.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep at the base of a sturdy trellis or pole in warm soil once frost danger has passed. Vigorous 5 to 8 foot vines are high-yielding and produce a bounty of 5 to 7 inch, white-seeded pods excellent fresh, canned, or frozen. Provide strong support before the vines begin to climb. Harvest pods while young and tender, picking every few days to keep the vines producing over a long season.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches at least 65 degrees.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm soil, planting in blocks of several short rows rather than one long row to ensure good pollination. This sugar-enhanced hybrid produces 8-inch ears packed with 16 rows of juicy golden kernels that hold their sweetness well after harvest. Sturdy stalks carry a strong disease package for consistent yields. Feed with nitrogen and water deeply during tasseling and ear fill, and harvest when silks brown and kernels are plump and milky.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or summer; this variety tolerates heat for extended sowing.

directions: Sow seed 1/4 inch deep in fertile soil and thin to about 4 inches apart, or grow in containers. This baby bok choy tolerates heat, so you can sow it through summer, harvesting at about 4 inches tall for delicate, crisp, mild-flavored leaves. The uniform green plants are an indispensable stir-fry ingredient and also good fresh or marinated. Keep soil evenly moist to keep growth tender, and harvest whole heads while young for the best texture.

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This classic heirloom, thought to date to the late 1800s, has distinctive potato-leaf foliage and produces massive 16 to 24 ounce pink fruits with rich, old-fashioned flavor. The indeterminate vines need a tall, sturdy stake or trellis and benefit from suckering for airflow. Water evenly at the base to reduce cracking, and harvest fruit when fully colored and slightly soft.

common_name: Brandywine Red & Yellow Blend Pole Tomato

planting.note: Start indoors 4 to 6 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Start seed indoors 4 to 6 weeks before the last frost and transplant deeply once nights stay warm. This blend pairs Red Brandywine, with its rich, well-balanced flavor, and golden Yellow Brandywine, which is sweet-tart, both producing large fruits often near a pound and sometimes close to two. The indeterminate vines need a tall, sturdy support and benefit from pruning for airflow. Water evenly to prevent cracking, and harvest each color when fully ripe and slightly soft.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring and again in late summer for a fall crop.

directions: Sow seed just 1/8 inch deep and barely cover, since lettuce needs light to germinate in cool soil. This variety holds its pretty leaves high above the core for easy cutting and forms compact heads that yield well in a small space. Strong bolt resistance extends the harvest, and it resists downy mildew, lettuce leaf aphid, and Fusarium wilt. Keep soil evenly moist, provide afternoon shade in warm weather, and harvest outer leaves or the whole head as needed.
